summary: I was born in Union, South Carolina, where my early education also began. My grandfather built a church and a school there, which was part of my family's legacy. Union is where my roots are, and it shaped much of my early life before I moved on to Salisbury for further education.

I was educated in Salisbury, North Carolina, where my mother taught English at Livingstone College. After my naval service, my wife and I made our home in Salisbury, which felt like coming back to my roots. Salisbury was an important place for my family and my personal development.

My father, Edward Muskelly, was an engineer for the Southern Railway and operated Engine #34. His work with the railway was a significant part of our family history and influenced my upbringing. I grew up hearing about his experiences and the importance of his job.

My mother was a professor of English at Livingstone College in Salisbury, North Carolina. She was a towering figure, both literally and figuratively, standing 6'6" and teaching many students. Her role at Livingstone College was a source of pride for our family.

I joined the United States Navy in 1950 with my parents' consent, and I was sent from Salisbury to San Diego for boot training. My Navy service included two and a half years in Korea aboard the USS Albany and later shore duty in Pensacola, Florida. The Navy shaped much of my early adult life and introduced me to my wife.

After enlisting in the Navy, I was shipped from Salisbury, North Carolina, to San Diego, California, for my boot training. San Diego was where my military journey officially began before being assigned to the USS Albany. It was a pivotal place marking the start of my naval career.

I served aboard the USS Albany, a heavy cruiser, during the Korean War for about two and a half years. It was my first assignment after boot training and where I spent much of my active duty. The ship took me into Korea, and later I returned to it when stationed in Norfolk.

During the Korean War, I served on the USS Albany and was deployed to Korea for two and a half years starting in 1950. This period was a defining chapter in my life, exposing me to the realities of war and military service. After the war, I was given shore duty in Pensacola, Florida.
cues: two and a half years in Korea

summary: After my time in Korea, I was assigned shore duty in Pensacola, Florida, at the Naval Air Station. It was there that I met my wife, Joyce Andrews, who worked as a carhop at Joe Martin's Barbecue. Pensacola was not only a military post for me but also the place where my personal life took a significant turn.

I was stationed at the Naval Air Station Pensacola during my shore duty starting in early 1953. This base was a key part of my Navy career after Korea, and it was near here that I met my future wife. The station represented a transition from sea duty to shore assignments.

Joyce and I were married in Louisville, Mississippi, in 1954. This was shortly after our courtship began in Pensacola, Florida. Louisville holds a special place in my heart as the site of our wedding and the start of our married life.

I was stationed at the Norfolk Naval Shipyard in Norfolk, Virginia, after leaving Pensacola in 1955. This was where I served for three years before being discharged from the Navy. Norfolk was the final military post of my naval career.

Norfolk, Virginia, was where I was stationed for three years after my time in Pensacola, and it was also where I was discharged from the Navy. After my discharge, my wife and I returned to Salisbury, North Carolina. Norfolk marked the end of my military service.

After my Navy discharge, I worked for the Veterans Affairs Medical Center for about a year and a half. This was my first civilian job before moving on to work in the steel industry. The VA Hospital was an important step in my post-military career.

I moved to Youngstown, Ohio, to work as a crane operator at the Youngstown Tubing Company after leaving the VA Hospital. I worked there for four years until a strike occurred. Youngstown was a significant part of my working life before relocating to Seattle.

The Youngstown Tubing Company was where I worked as a crane operator for four years in Ohio. It was a steel mill, and I was employed there until a strike disrupted operations. This job was part of my transition from healthcare work to industrial labor.

In 1958, my wife and I moved to Seattle after she visited and loved the city. I began working at the US Public Health Service Hospital there in 1962, starting as a janitor and moving up to engineer. Seattle became our home for many years and the center of my union and healthcare activism.
cues: worked at US Public Health Service Hospital

entity: United States Public Health Service Hospital
summary: I started working at the US Public Health Service Hospital in Seattle in 1962 as a janitor and was promoted to engineer within eight months. I became involved in the hospital's union and later helped lead efforts to save the hospital from closure. The hospital was central to my work in healthcare advocacy and union leadership.

I met David Loud around 1967 or 1968 when we began working together on healthcare issues in Seattle. Along with Tommy Byers and others, we formed a coalition to fight for the Public Health Service Hospital. David was one of the young, ambitious people committed to improving healthcare access.

Tommy Byers was another key figure I met in the late 1960s as we worked together on healthcare reform and saving the Public Health Service Hospital. He was part of the group that formed the Public Healthcare Coalition with me and David Loud. Tommy played an instrumental role in our long fight to keep the hospital open.

Dr. Willard Johnson became the director of the Public Health Service Hospital and was a great leader who understood human needs. He was from Texas and asked for my help as union president to support healthcare for people without resources. Willard was instrumental in opening the hospital to free clinics and building community support.

Dr. Willard Johnson, who became director of the Public Health Service Hospital, was from Texas. His Southern background helped him connect with me, and he brought a humble, human touch to his leadership. Texas was part of his identity and influenced his approach to healthcare.

Donald Fleets was a friend who encouraged me to get involved in the union at the Public Health Service Hospital. He warned me about the risks, but with my wife's support, I ran for union president and won. Donald's encouragement was a key factor in my union leadership.

Bob Kaplan was the lawyer who worked with our group during the fight to save the Public Health Service Hospital. He was Jewish and part of the coalition that included myself, Tommy Byers, and David Loud. Bob helped us navigate legal and political challenges in our advocacy.

The Public Healthcare Coalition was formed by myself, Tommy Byers, David Loud, and others to fight the closure of the Public Health Service Hospital. We brought together many beneficiary groups and worked for over ten years to keep the hospital open. The coalition was central to our community-based healthcare advocacy.

The Reagan administration was the federal government entity that ultimately succeeded in closing the Public Health Service Hospitals in 1981. Despite our decade-long fight, the administration pushed through closures, but we managed to negotiate for local control through the Pacific Hospital Preservation & Development Authority. The Reagan years were challenging for our healthcare efforts.

The Pacific Hospital Preservation & Development Authority was created by the city of Seattle to take over operation of the Public Health Service Hospital after federal closure in 1981. Tommy Byers was instrumental in its creation, and I worked closely with him and others on the steering committee. This authority helped continue healthcare services locally.

Senator Warren Magnuson, known as Maggie, was the greatest senator of my time and a strong supporter of the hospital and its employees. He chaired the Senate Appropriations Committee and helped fund programs like Upward Mobility for hospital workers. I got to know him well and admired his leadership and humanity.

The United States Senate Appropriations Committee, led by Senator Warren Magnuson during my time, was crucial in funding the Public Health Service Hospital and its employee education programs. Magnuson's leadership on this committee helped secure resources for the hospital and its workers. The committee's support was vital to our efforts.

The Catholic Seamen's Club in Seattle was a place where Senator Magnuson and I socialized during events. I remember once having to get vodka for Maggie there, and a policeman even escorted me back to the club. It was part of the community where we connected with local leaders and supporters.

Charles Royer was the mayor of Seattle, and we hosted a party for him at our home. Our house was a gathering place for political and community leaders, including Charles and members of the Washington delegation. Royer's mayoralty coincided with our healthcare advocacy efforts in Seattle.
